skill.name: mcp-builder
skill.description: Deprecated mcp-use guide for building Model Context Protocol servers. It explains tools, resources, prompts, response helpers, resource templates, and React widgets, while recommending mcp-app-builder for new work.
skill.summary: Build MCP servers, resources, prompts, and widgets with mcp-use patterns.
skill.author: mcp-use

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and critical patterns, but review found they are mostly Markdown code examples, method names, license text, and documentation snippets rather than executable skill behavior. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found. The skill still carries medium risk because copied examples include environment secrets, network calls, and file reads that need access controls in generated MCP servers.

medium_findings.title: Copyable API Key in URL Query Example
medium_findings.description: The documentation shows an API key read from an environment variable and placed into a URL query string. If copied into a real MCP server, the key could be exposed through logs, caches, browser history, or upstream analytics.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact example reads WEATHER_API_KEY and interpolates it into a fetch URL. It is documentation rather than active code, so the risk depends on a user copying the pattern.

medium_findings.title: Unconstrained File Resource Template Example
medium_findings.description: The resource template example maps a user-controlled path into a file read without showing allow-listing, path normalization, or workspace boundaries. If copied directly, a generated MCP server could expose unintended local files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example directly connects a URI path parameter to readFile(path). It is a sample snippet, but the missing boundary checks are a realistic implementation risk.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts align with Markdown headings, prose, method names, or license text. No evidence found of cryptographic APIs, hash functions, password handling, or encryption code in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found documentation text and no executable cryptographic logic. The repeated alerts are consistent with token-level matches in Markdown and license prose.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Matches Are Mostly Markdown
low_findings.description: Most command execution alerts come from code fences, inline method names, and eval instructions. The only direct shell command is a visible npx install command for the replacement skill, not hidden execution by this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced files are Markdown or JSON evaluation text. No script file, package hook, or automatic command execution path was found.

low_findings.title: Critical Combined Heuristic Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The scanner combined code examples for environment variables, network calls, and command-like text into a critical heuristic. Review found no data exfiltration flow, hidden network endpoint, or executable automation inside the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious categories are present only as guidance for MCP apps. The skill content does not itself run code or transmit collected secrets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret in Query String Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The weather API example places an API key into a URL query parameter. Generated code should prefer provider-supported authorization headers or other non-URL secret transport.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit in a code example and is security-sensitive when reused. It is not active code in this skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: User Path to File Read Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The resource template example reads a path derived from a URI parameter. Generated servers should constrain paths to an allow-listed base directory and reject traversal.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path-to-readFile flow is visible in the example. It remains a documentation risk rather than a confirmed vulnerability in this skill package.

content.value_statement: MCP server projects can become hard to structure when tools, resources, prompts, and widgets overlap. This skill gives mcp-use patterns for designing and implementing those parts consistently.
actual_capabilities: Guides planning for MCP tools, resources, prompts, and widgets.
Shows TypeScript patterns for server.tool, server.resource, server.resourceTemplate, and server.prompt.
Explains mcp-use response helpers for text, objects, Markdown, HTML, binary data, errors, mixed output, and widgets.
Documents React widget structure with widgetMetadata, McpUseProvider, useWidget, persistent state, and tool calls.
Provides evaluation scenarios for architecture, implementation, skill usage, and widget behavior.
limitations: The skill is deprecated and recommends mcp-app-builder for new work.
It provides guidance and examples, not a standalone runnable generator.
Several examples use mock data or placeholder service calls.
Security controls for generated MCP servers must be added by the implementer.

use_cases.title: Create a focused MCP tool server
use_cases.description: Use the skill to define tool schemas, response helpers, errors, and server startup patterns for a TypeScript MCP server.

use_cases.title: Standardize MCP architecture choices
use_cases.description: Use the design reference to decide when a feature should be a tool, resource, prompt, or widget.

use_cases.title: Prototype interactive MCP widgets
use_cases.description: Use the widget reference to build React UI components that receive tool output and support interaction.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic MCP tool
prompt_templates.prompt: Use mcp-builder to create a simple MCP tool for [task]. Include a clear Zod schema, helpful descriptions, and a text response.

prompt_templates.title: Add a resource and prompt
prompt_templates.prompt: Use mcp-builder to add a read-only resource for [data] and a reusable prompt for [workflow]. Explain why each part is the right MCP primitive.

prompt_templates.title: Design a widget flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use mcp-builder to design a widget tool for [user flow]. Include the tool inputs, widget props, loading state, and model-visible output.

prompt_templates.title: Review an MCP implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use mcp-builder to review my MCP server for tool boundaries, resource templates, widget registration, response helpers, error handling, and security gaps.

output_examples.input: Create a weather widget for an MCP server.
output_examples.output: A plan with one weather tool, one matching widget, clear input fields, mock weather data, loading behavior, and a concise model-visible summary.

output_examples.input: Add a resource for user profiles.
output_examples.output: A resource design with a stable URI pattern, JSON response format, parameter validation guidance, and notes about access boundaries.

output: Issues with overly broad tools or duplicated widgets.
Recommendations for validation, errors, and safer API key handling.

best_practices: Start with the design reference before writing code so each MCP primitive has a clear purpose.
Use descriptive Zod schemas, explicit annotations, and graceful error responses for every tool.
Keep secrets in environment variables and avoid sending them in URLs or widget props.
anti_patterns: Using this deprecated skill when mcp-app-builder is available for new work.
Creating one broad tool that performs unrelated actions instead of focused tools.
Copying file or network examples without path limits, authentication, and secret-handling controls.

faq.question: Is this skill still current?
faq.answer: No. The skill description says it is deprecated and recommends mcp-app-builder for new projects.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps design and implement mcp-use MCP servers with tools, resources, prompts, response helpers, and React widgets.

faq.question: Does it generate a complete working server by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and references that an AI coding assistant can use while editing your project.

faq.question: Can it help with interactive UI widgets?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents widget files, widgetMetadata, McpUseProvider, useWidget, persistent state, and widget tool configuration.

faq.question: Does it require API keys?
faq.answer: The skill itself does not require keys. Some generated server examples may use environment variables for third-party APIs.

faq.question: What security checks should I add to generated servers?
faq.answer: Validate inputs, constrain file paths, avoid secrets in URLs, document environment variables, and mark destructive tools clearly.
